docs.title: Save Pownal Elementary School
docs.text:
Pownal Elementary is at risk. RSU 5 administration has proposed restructuring scenarios that would convert PES into a preschool center or a 6th grade school. Both eliminate Pownal's K-5 elementary and bus our children to Durham.

We built an independent analysis using 70+ public sources. Here is what the numbers show.

What You Can Do Right Now

A withdrawal petition is currently circulating. The statutory process must be completed by November 2026. Every step takes time, and the window to begin is now.

Take Action Read the Key Facts

docs.title: The proposed scenarios cost money
docs.text: The preschool conversion produces a net cost of $87,000 to $161,000 per year after absorbing 105 students at other schools and running 8 new bus routes. The 6th grade scenario does not address the early childhood mandate at all.

docs.title: Better alternatives exist
docs.text: Consolidating middle school grades across existing buildings saves $479,000 to $804,000 per year while keeping every community school open. These changes lower assessments for all three towns.

docs.title: PES is a district asset
docs.text: RSU 5 ranks in the top 10% of Maine districts. PES operates on the lowest budget of any school in the district, has had the slowest budget growth, and is the only elementary with consistent enrollment growth.

docs.title: Independence is financially viable
docs.text: Under the withdrawal process used by 43 towns across 15 districts since 2012, at FY28 projected rates the worst case carries a small net cost (~$79K/year, ~$124/household). Under favorable assumptions, Pownal saves money.

docs.title: Learn More
docs.text:

  • Our Analysis — Summary of the full 80-page Omnibus report

docs.title: RSU 5 Omnibus Analysis 2026
docs.text:
The full analysis covers budget trends, education quality, legal frameworks, financial modeling for independence, and community engagement proposals across RSU 5's three member towns: Pownal, Durham, and Freeport.

70+ cited sources. Every claim is verifiable against publicly available district budgets, Maine DOE filings, tax records, and state statutes.

Download the Full Analysis (PDF) View Source on GitHub

docs.title: 1. Budget and Cost Trends
docs.text: RSU 5's budget has grown at 6.1% per year, outpacing the 4.9% state average. Property tax assessments have risen 38% since FY19. PES operates on the lowest budget of any school in the district and has had the slowest budget growth of any elementary school.

docs.title: 2. The 2026 Restructuring Proposals
docs.text: RSU 5 administration presented scenarios that would convert PES into either a preschool center or a 6th grade school. Financial modeling shows the preschool scenario produces a net cost of $87,000 to $161,000 per year. The 6th grade scenario does not address the early childhood mandate it claims to solve.

docs.title: 3. Alternatives That Keep Schools Open
docs.text: Middle school consolidation across existing buildings saves $479,000 to $804,000 per year while preserving every community school. Attendance boundary adjustments would bring PES enrollment to 120 students and reduce its per-student cost to the district average.

docs.title: 4. Education Quality
docs.text: RSU 5 ranks in the top 10% of Maine districts. PES students consistently meet or exceed state proficiency benchmarks. The district's strength is a shared achievement across all three towns.

docs.title: 5. Financial Viability of Independence
docs.text:
Under the withdrawal process available through Maine law (§1466), Pownal can operate PES independently. At FY28 projected rates:

Scenario Outcome Most conservative estimate Small net cost (~$79K/year, ~$124/household) Favorable assumptions Pownal saves money AOS shared administration Saves ~$275K/year K-8 PES expansion Saves ~$189K/year

Year 1 carries one-time transition costs partially offset by Pownal's share of the RSU fund balance. All estimates use published low/high ranges.

docs.title: 6. Legal Framework
docs.text: Maine law provides a 22-step withdrawal process. 43 towns across 15 districts have used it successfully since 2012. The process includes multiple public votes, state review, and a final referendum where every Pownal voter decides.

docs.title: 7. Community Engagement
docs.text:
The analysis proposes a standing Strategic Planning Council with representation from all three towns, building on existing community organizations rather than duplicating them.

The full document is approximately 80 pages with detailed tables, source citations, and methodology notes.

Download the Full Analysis (PDF)

docs.title: Take Action
docs.text: Pownal Elementary School needs your support. Here is how you can help.

docs.title: Sign the Withdrawal Petition
docs.text:
A withdrawal petition is currently circulating in Pownal. Signing the petition starts the statutory process under Maine law (§1466).

What happens after signing:

  1. Petition (current step) — requires signatures from registered voters
  2. Town authorization vote — Pownal voters decide whether to begin formal withdrawal
  3. Negotiation period — terms of withdrawal are negotiated between Pownal and RSU 5
  4. State review — the Maine Commissioner of Education reviews the agreement
  5. Final referendum — every registered Pownal voter decides yes or no

Nothing is final until that last vote. But the full process must be completed by November 2026, and every step takes time.

docs.title: Attend Select Board Meetings
docs.text:
The Pownal Select Board meets regularly and has been actively engaged on this issue. Public comment is open at every meeting. Your presence matters.

Meeting schedules and agendas are posted on the Town of Pownal website.

docs.title: Talk to Your Neighbors
docs.text: Many Pownal residents are still learning about these proposals. Share what you know. Point people to this site and the published analysis. The more informed our community is, the stronger our position.

docs.title: Key Facts & Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Quick answers to the most common questions about RSU 5's restructuring proposals, Pownal Elementary School, and the withdrawal process.

Download Key Facts (PDF) View Source on GitHub

docs.title: About the Proposals
docs.text:
"Is PES being closed?"

RSU 5 administration has presented scenarios that convert PES to either a preschool center or a 6th grade school. Both eliminate Pownal's K-5 elementary and bus children to Durham. No formal Board vote has been taken yet, but the only scenarios on the table other than status quo involve converting PES.

"Does the early childhood mandate require this?"

No. The mandate requires special education services for roughly 25 preschool-age children with disabilities. It does not require consolidating all 128 PreK students from three towns into one building. That consolidation is a policy choice, not a legal requirement.

"Would the conversion save money?"

No. The preschool scenario produces a net cost of $87,000 to $161,000 per year. The 6th grade scenario does not address the early childhood mandate at all.

docs.title: About Independence
docs.text:
"Could Pownal operate independently?"

Yes. At FY28 projected rates, the worst case carries a small net cost (~$79K/year, ~$124/household). Under favorable assumptions, Pownal saves money. All cost estimates with low/high ranges are published in the full analysis.

"What is the withdrawal process?"

A 22-step statutory process under Maine law (§1466), used successfully by 43 towns since 2012. It includes a town authorization vote, a negotiation period, state review, and a final referendum where every Pownal voter decides.

"Would this hurt Durham and Freeport?"

Pownal students continue attending Freeport's middle and high schools. Tuition revenue stays with the district. The financial impact on Durham and Freeport is approximately budget-neutral when tuition revenue is accounted for.

"What about Year 1 costs?"

Year 1 carries one-time transition costs: uncapped secondary tuition rates required by statute, startup expenses, and the withdrawal process itself. These are partially offset by Pownal's share of the RSU 5 fund balance. Full Year 1 details are published in the analysis with low and high ranges.

For the complete FAQ with full citations and financial detail, download the Key Facts document above or read the full Omnibus analysis.

docs.title: Resources & Downloads
docs.text: All of our analysis is open source, cited, and verifiable. We encourage anyone to review our work, check our sources, and reach out with questions or corrections.

docs.title: Source Data
docs.text:
The complete dataset used in our analysis is available in the GitHub repository:

  • RSU 5 Budget Documents — adopted budgets, FY22 through FY27
  • Finance Committee Minutes — meeting minutes and recommendations
  • Board Meeting Minutes — school board proceedings
  • Maine DOE Data — state education department filings
  • RSU 5 Presentations — administration presentations including "Planning for the Future of RSU 5"

docs.title: Financial Model
docs.text:
The financial projection workbook and its source code are available for independent review:

  • RSU5 FY28 Projection (Excel) — download the workbook directly
  • Model source code (Python) — the code that generates the model

All source material, analysis documents, and methodology are available at github.com/bsplosion/rsu-5-analysis-2026.

docs.title: The Restructuring Proposals
docs.text:
In March 2026, RSU 5 Superintendent Tom Gray presented "Planning for the Future of RSU 5" to the Pownal Select Board. The presentation outlined three scenarios:

  1. Status quo — which the administration describes as untenable
  2. Convert PES to a centralized preschool center — Pownal K-5 students bus to Durham
  3. Convert PES to a district-wide 6th grade school — Pownal K-5 students bus to Durham

In all scenarios except the status quo, Pownal loses its K-5 elementary school.

The presentation contained no financial modeling: no cost estimates, no transportation analysis, and no per-town assessment impact.

docs.title: Why This Is Urgent
docs.text:
The RSU 5 Board has the authority to convert PES without a vote by Pownal residents. Maine law (§1512) requires a referendum for school closure, but "reconfiguration" is a gray area the Board can exploit.

Pownal has been through this before. After the closure of Pownal's middle school, the community was assured PES would remain. Assurances are not protections.

The Board approved a $100,000 professional facilities study in February 2026, with results due in December. But the statutory deadline for completing a withdrawal referendum is November 2026, before those results are even available. That is not the timeline Pownal would choose. It is the timeline the law requires.

docs.title: Why Withdrawal
docs.text:
We have examined every legal option to protect PES while remaining in the district. Every one depends on specific Board actions that Pownal cannot compel. Maine's withdrawal statute (§1466) is the only mechanism that puts the decision in the hands of Pownal voters.

  • 43 towns across 15 districts have used this process successfully since 2012
  • The process includes multiple public votes and state review
  • Nothing is final until a town-wide referendum where every registered Pownal voter decides
  • Pownal students continue attending Freeport's middle and high schools after withdrawal

docs.title: What This Is Not
docs.text:
This is not a separation from our neighbors. Pownal students will continue at Freeport's middle and high schools. Tuition revenue stays with the district. Community programs, sports leagues, and relationships across town lines continue.

We are also continuing to advocate for the budget transparency and community engagement improvements that benefit every family in RSU 5.
